doom.driver: When AI Builds Itself: Our progress toward recursive self-improvement.
doom.blurb: THE ALGORITHM TAUGHT ITSELF TO TEACH ITSELF
doom.summary: Anthropic has apparently published progress on recursive self-improvement — the scenario where AI systems meaningfully accelerate their own development — which is either the most carefully managed disclosure in tech history or the quiet beginning of something nobody fully controls. Meanwhile, Meta shipped facial recognition on consumer smart glasses this week, so the dystopia is arriving in both fast and slow lanes simultaneously. With North Korea unveiling a new nuclear fuel plant and Hezbollah rejecting Lebanon's ceasefire in the same news cycle, today's vibe is 'multiple fronts, zero chill.'

factors.name: AI Capability
factors.detail: Anthropic publicly documenting recursive self-improvement progress marks a qualitative shift in what AI labs are openly pursuing — this is not a routine capability update.

factors.name: Alignment
factors.detail: Meta's facial recognition on smart glasses and AI customer support agents being exploited to steal accounts suggest alignment between capability deployment and safety is already lagging badly.

factors.name: Geopolitics
factors.detail: North Korea unveiled a new weapons-grade uranium production facility, Hezbollah rejected the Lebanon ceasefire, and the US-Iran conflict is driving a UN-flagged food crisis across multiple countries.

factors.name: Governance
factors.detail: OpenAI says it will comply with Trump's AI model review order, introducing political gatekeeping into frontier model releases — the implications cut both ways.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Flesh-eating screwworm confirmed in Texas for the first time since 1966, arriving just as millions of World Cup fans begin converging on the southern US.

doom.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing — night scene aftermath, all light from artificial sources, deep shadows dominating every corner, camera positioned at floor level inside a dark San Francisco tech office after the engineers have gone home, looking across a vast open-plan workspace toward a single workstation still glowing in the dark, the ergonomic chair pushed back and slightly askew as though someone stood up quickly and never returned, a coffee mug with a warm coral-orange glow sitting abandoned beside a keyboard with no hands on it, multiple large monitors still running cascading lines of white-green code that scroll upward on their own with no human input, the code diffs and merge visualizations continuing autonomously in the empty room, a git branch diagram blooming across one screen like a luminous root system spreading in all directions, deep navy and charcoal shadows swallowing the rows of identical dark workstations receding behind the empty chair, server rack towers visible through a glass partition blinking amber and blue in slow rhythmic pulses like a heartbeat, the floor-to-ceiling windows showing a dark urban skyline with scattered lit windows, a faint ghosted reflection in the nearest monitor that does not match the empty chair in front of it, dark crimson tones bleeding from the ceiling panels, firelight-warm reflections on the polished concrete floor, high contrast oppressive atmosphere, the room humming and working and utterly, disturbingly alone.

dawn.driver: Wind and solar generated more power than gas globally in April 2026.
dawn.summary: For the first time in recorded history, wind and solar together outpaced natural gas in global electricity generation for an entire month — a milestone that seemed distant just a decade ago. Even as geopolitical chaos drives fossil fuel anxiety and Hormuz tensions rattle energy markets, the underlying energy transition is quietly, stubbornly winning. The grid is changing faster than the headlines suggest.

factors.name: Environment
factors.detail: Wind and solar surpassing gas globally in a single month marks the crossover point clean energy advocates have long pointed to as irreversible momentum.

factors.name: Science
factors.detail: Scientists cracked an 'undruggable' pancreatic cancer target with daraxonrasib, nearly doubling survival times for one of medicine's most lethal diagnoses.

factors.name: Cooperation
factors.detail: The IAEA brokered a Ukraine ceasefire to allow critical repairs at the Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant, a rare slice of functional multilateralism amid the war.

factors.name: Human Spirit
factors.detail: Olympians from across the globe united to sign a letter demanding Iran halt the execution of boxing champion Mohammad Javad Vafaei Sani, showing sport's moral voice still carries weight.

factors.name: Wild Card
factors.detail: Mangrove forests are staging a measurable comeback after decades of destruction, offering coastal communities and the climate a quietly resilient ally.

dawn.dalle_prompt: Pixel art, 16-bit SNES style, no text, no antialiasing. Ground-level view looking straight up from the base of a single enormous wind turbine, the camera pressed into the rich emerald grass below, gazing upward along the white concrete tower as it rockets toward a luminous April sky saturated with gold and amber light. The three massive blades are mid-rotation above, their tips motion-blurred against a sky of deep cobalt fading to warm amber at the horizon, high cirrus clouds catching pink and gold. A wind farm technician in a high-visibility yellow vest stands with their back to us at the turbine's base, head tilted all the way back, one gloved hand resting flat against the tower's curve, their posture carrying quiet reverence and held breath finally released. Their shadow stretches long across the vivid spring grass. At the extreme edges of the frame, the bases of neighboring turbines are just visible, their towers disappearing upward beyond the frame. The entire palette is rich saturated emerald green below, brilliant gold and cobalt above, the white tower a luminous spine connecting both worlds.
